title: Acute diverticulitis (LLQ)
vignette: 73-year-old with 10 days of LLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis (WBC 15k), palpable LLQ mass; aunt had colon cancer. Most likely cause of pain?

clues.note: Acute infectious/inflammatory process, not indolent tumor.
Weak family history; bait toward cancer.

whatMatters: Elderly + LLQ pain + fever + leukocytosis + mass = acute diverticulitis ('left-sided appendicitis').
strategy: Acute + febrile = infection (diverticulitis); chronic + weight loss/anemia = cancer. Confirm with CT, not acute colonoscopy.

eliminations.why: Cancer is chronic (weight loss, anemia); one aunt is weak FH.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient had 6 months of weight loss, melena, strong family history, and age >50 (colorectal cancer)

eliminations.why: Mass is LLQ, no groin bulge.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient had a reducible groin mass that enlarged with Valsalva (inguinal hernia)
Patient had sudden-onset hematochezia at the watershed area after a hypotensive episode (ischemic colitis)
Patient was a young sexually active woman with bilateral adnexal tenderness and cervical motion tenderness (PID)

eliminations.why: Not a new focal mass in elderly.
eliminations.correctIf: Patient had years of bloody diarrhea, cramping, and skip lesions on colonoscopy (IBD)

reasoning: LLQ + mass in older adult = acute diverticulitis -> A
mechanism: Diverticula obstruct -> microperforation and inflammation (sigmoid). LLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis, +/- mass. CT diagnosis; complications: abscess, colovesical fistula (pneumaturia), obstruction, perforation.
memoryHook: Left-sided appendicitis — acute + febrile = infection, not cancer.
